name: Garlic
summary: A pungent allium whose sulfur compound allicin has meta-analysed evidence for modestly lowering blood pressure.
superfoodReason: Its organosulfur compounds, chiefly allicin, have randomized-trial evidence for reducing blood pressure and cholesterol.

benefits.mechanism: Crushing garlic forms allicin, which is converted to hydrogen-sulfide and nitric-oxide signalling molecules that relax blood vessels. A meta-analysis of randomized trials found garlic lowered systolic and diastolic blood pressure, with the largest effect in people who were hypertensive.

citations.title: Effect of garlic on blood pressure: a meta-analysis (Wang et al., J Clin Hypertens 2015)

surprisingFacts.text: Allicin doesn't exist in an intact clove — it's created when garlic is crushed or chopped and two compounds meet. Letting crushed garlic rest ~10 minutes before cooking lets more allicin form and survive the heat.

cautions.effect: Beyond the obvious breath and body odour, a lot of raw garlic can irritate the stomach and cause heartburn. Garlic also has a mild blood-thinning effect, which matters mostly alongside anticoagulants or before surgery.

citations.title: Allicin, the Odor of Freshly Crushed Garlic: A Review of Recent Progress in Understanding Allicin's Effects on Cells (Borlinghaus et al., Molecules 2021)
